data.title: OpenAI’s GPT-5.2: A Leap in Professional AI Capabilities
data.description:
“We have a new model that is significantly stronger than anything that we’ve released before. It’s better at creating spreadsheets, building presentations, perceiving images, writing code, and understanding long context.” This declaration from MacKenzie Sigalos of CNBC, reporting on OpenAI’s latest announcement, sets the stage for a significant advancement in AI technology. The unveiling of GPT-5.2 signals a strategic push towards professional applications, aiming to integrate more seamlessly into the workflows of businesses and individual professionals.

Fidji Simo, CEO of Applications at OpenAI, spoke with Sigalos about the new model. Simo highlighted that GPT-5.2 is the company’s “most advanced model yet for everyday professional use.” This statement underscores a targeted approach, focusing on practical, day-to-day tasks that professionals encounter. The improvements are not merely incremental; they are designed to enhance productivity and efficiency across a range of demanding applications.

A core insight from the announcement is the model’s enhanced ability to handle complex, multi-step tasks. This is particularly evident in its improved performance in areas like coding and document analysis. For professionals who rely on these functions, GPT-5.2 promises a more robust and reliable AI assistant. The reduction in “hallucinations,” or the generation of incorrect or nonsensical information, is another critical development. This addresses a persistent challenge with earlier AI models, fostering greater trust and utility for professional users.

The video description further elaborates on these advancements, stating that GPT-5.2 is “better at creating spreadsheets, building presentations, perceiving images, writing code and understanding long context.” This comprehensive upgrade suggests a more versatile and powerful tool. The ability to process and understand extensive amounts of text is particularly noteworthy, as it allows the AI to maintain context over longer interactions and more complex documents. This is crucial for tasks such as summarizing lengthy reports, drafting detailed proposals, or engaging in extended coding projects.

Simo also touched upon the strategic importance of the new model, noting that its development has been “months in the making.” This indicates a deliberate and iterative process, aimed at refining the AI’s capabilities. She further elaborated, “under the hood, this new phase of the company looks more like incremental tune-ups than moonshot bets.” This perspective suggests a maturation of OpenAI’s development strategy, focusing on steady, significant improvements rather than revolutionary leaps, which can lead to more predictable and reliable performance.

The announcement of GPT-5.2 coincides with another significant development: a strategic partnership with Disney. This collaboration includes a $1 billion equity investment in OpenAI from Disney and curated Sora videos being available on Disney+. This partnership is a testament to the growing recognition of AI’s potential in creative industries. The ability for Sora users to create videos with Disney’s copyrighted characters opens up new avenues for content creation and intellectual property utilization, albeit with potential legal and ethical considerations that will likely emerge.

The integration of GPT-5.2 into professional workflows is poised to redefine how tasks are approached. Its enhanced accuracy and contextual understanding can free up professionals to focus on higher-level strategic thinking and problem-solving. This iterative improvement model, as described by Simo, suggests a commitment to continuous refinement, ensuring that the AI remains a valuable and evolving asset for its users. The company’s focus on practical applications signals a clear understanding of the market’s needs.

data.title: AI Race Redefines Battleground: Beyond Model Superiority
data.description:
The current debate on CNBC’s “The Exchange” underscored a critical evolution in the artificial intelligence landscape, moving beyond mere model superiority to a multifaceted competition centered on strategic market focus, distribution channels, and computational power. This shift signals a maturing industry where raw algorithmic prowess, while essential, is increasingly just one component of a winning strategy.

Alex Kantrowitz, founder of Big Technology and a CNBC contributor, and Gil Luria, an analyst at D.A. Davidson, engaged with host Kelly Evans about OpenAI’s recent GPT-5.2 model announcement and its broader implications for the fiercely contested AI market. Their discussion illuminated the strategic maneuvers underway among leading AI developers, highlighting the intricate factors that will determine future dominance.

Kantrowitz highlighted OpenAI’s significant pivot from a consumer-centric approach to an “all in on enterprise” strategy. He revealed that Sam Altman, OpenAI’s CEO, has been actively meeting with heads of New York publications, emphasizing enterprise as a “massive priority” for the company in 2026. This strategic redirection is compelling, given that the enterprise AI category is projected to surge from virtually zero in 2022 to a staggering $37 billion next year, according to data firm Gartner. This anticipated growth makes the enterprise sector an undeniable battleground for AI leaders.

Kelly Evans, the interviewer, challenged this enterprise focus, asserting that the fundamental issue remains “who has the better model.” She invoked Marc Benioff’s recent tweet, which starkly articulated a user’s preference: “…I’ve used ChatGPT every day for 3 years. Just spent 2 hours on Gemini 3. I’m not going back…” This anecdote powerfully illustrates the tangible impact of perceived model superiority on individual user experience and loyalty.

However, Kantrowitz countered, asserting that the true differentiator is not solely the model’s inherent intelligence. “I think right now what matters is what you do with that model and how you distribute it,” he stated. This perspective elevates distribution as a decisive factor, suggesting that even a slightly inferior model, if widely accessible and seamlessly integrated, can outperform a technically superior but poorly distributed one.

This point spotlights Google’s formidable advantage in the AI race. With its vast ecosystem of products—from search to Android to Workspace—and the leadership of Sundar Pichai, whom Kantrowitz dubbed “the king of distribution,” Google possesses an inherent, almost insurmountable, competitive edge. Even if Google’s models are merely “at par or even a little worse than OpenAI’s,” their unparalleled ability to integrate AI seamlessly into existing products and reach billions of users provides a critical advantage that startups and even well-funded competitors struggle to replicate.

Gil Luria largely echoed Kantrowitz, stating there isn’t “not that much of a difference” between current leading models, and future iterations like GPT-5.2 will likely offer only “slightly better” experiences. He broadened the discussion to include user experience design and, crucially, computational resources. Google’s access to immense compute power stands in stark contrast to OpenAI, which, Luria noted, “is still struggling to get compute because it needs capital to buy that compute…” This computational divide directly impacts model speed, efficiency, and accessibility, thereby profoundly influencing user satisfaction and the ability to scale.

The panelists agreed that the AI race is still in its nascent stages, despite the rapid advancements. Luria pointed out that “Grock will have something to say, Meta will have something to say,” underscoring the dynamic nature of the market and the impending entry of new, well-resourced players. Furthermore, established tech giants like Microsoft and Amazon are actively ensuring their participation in corporate AI through strategic partnerships and cloud infrastructure, adding more layers to the competitive landscape. The competition is not a static sprint but an evolving marathon with multiple entrants and shifting finish lines.

The discussion on “The Exchange” paints a picture of an AI sector rapidly maturing beyond its foundational research phase. The path to dominance will be paved not just by developing smarter algorithms, but by executing smarter business strategies that encompass strategic market targeting, widespread distribution, user-centric design, and the immense, capital-intensive power of computational infrastructure. The future of AI will be shaped by those who can master this complex interplay of innovation, access, and experience.

data.title: Gary Cohn: AI is a Productivity Enhancer, Not Today’s Labor Market Foe
data.description:
Gary Cohn, IBM Vice Chairman and former director of the National Economic Council, recently offered a compelling counter-narrative to the prevailing anxieties surrounding artificial intelligence and its impact on the labor market. Speaking on CNBC’s ‘Money Movers,’ Cohn posited that the current economic softness and job market challenges are rooted in traditional macroeconomic pressures, not the burgeoning wave of AI. His insights offer a nuanced perspective for founders, venture capitalists, and AI professionals grappling with the long-term implications of this transformative technology.

The discussion, featuring Cohn alongside CNBC’s Sara Eisen and Jon Fortt, delved into the Federal Reserve’s recent rate cuts, the state of the labor market, and the much-debated role of AI. Cohn articulated what he termed “the great dilemma” facing the Fed: persistent, sticky inflation hovering at 2.8%—well above their target—coupled with an increasingly soft employment market. He noted, “We still have sticky inflation… and we have an employment market that is soft, and I think the Fed Chair yesterday admitted that it may even be softer than we think it is.” This economic quandary, Cohn argued, is where the true pressure on labor originates.

Cohn emphasized that companies are currently facing margin pressures. Unable to fully pass increased input costs onto consumers, businesses are tightening their belts elsewhere. The primary variable being squeezed, he observed, is labor. This strategic cost-cutting, rather than technological displacement, explains the significant layoffs seen across various human-intensive industries. He starkly stated, “When you look at the companies that have downsized and announced the 50,000 layoffs and 20,000 layoffs, it’s not AI enhanced.” This direct challenge to the popular narrative underscores a critical distinction between cyclical economic adjustments and structural technological shifts.

Artificial intelligence, in Cohn’s view, is fundamentally a “productivity enhancer.” He firmly believes that “AI that we are seeing today is not causing the labor issues.” Instead, he foresees AI leading to job growth, not decline. This perspective suggests that while certain tasks may be automated, the overall economic pie expands, creating new roles and opportunities that didn’t exist before. The narrative shifts from replacement to augmentation, where AI empowers human workers to achieve more, faster, and with greater efficiency.

The interviewers pressed Cohn on this point, highlighting that past technological tools were used by humans, whereas AI seems poised to replace them to a greater degree. Cohn conceded that there would be “bumps” and adjustments, but maintained his optimistic outlook. He drew a powerful historical parallel, asserting, “There’s not been a seismic technological change in this world that hasn’t grown the size of the economy and hasn’t grown the size of employment.” He cited examples ranging from the cotton gin and the internal combustion engine to the internet, all of which, despite initial disruptions, ultimately led to broader economic expansion and increased job opportunities.

Cohn elaborated on the enhancement aspect, explaining that AI will take over mundane or undesirable tasks, freeing up human capital for more creative, complex, or client-facing roles. He reminisced about his early days at Goldman Sachs, where a dedicated person on each floor printed daily memos detailing meeting schedules and delivering mail. This role, now entirely obsolete due to email and digital calendars, did not result in a net loss of jobs but rather a reallocation of human effort to more productive endeavors within a growing firm. The economy simply became bigger, and employment expanded, albeit with different types of jobs.

This perspective offers valuable clarity for leaders in the startup ecosystem and tech insiders. It suggests that while the immediate future may see some turbulence as industries adapt to AI, the long-term trajectory is one of economic expansion and job creation. The focus for businesses should be on how to leverage AI to enhance productivity and create new value, rather than solely viewing it as a tool for cost reduction through workforce cuts. The true impact of AI, as Cohn argues, will be in its capacity to make humans more productive, ultimately fostering a larger, more dynamic economy.

data.title: Disney bets $1 billion on OpenAI’s Sora as Altman shrugs off Gemini threat
data.description:
“We are very focused on having the cheapest cost per token at the highest level of intelligence.” This declaration by OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, as reported by CNBC’s MacKenzie Sigalos, encapsulates the company’s strategic pivot from broad consumer appeal to a more targeted, performance-driven approach in the escalating AI arms race. The recent $1 billion investment from Disney, while a significant splashy content play, is framed by Altman not as the primary objective, but as a consequence of OpenAI’s commitment to advancing its core technology.

Sigalos spoke with Altman about the implications of this major deal and the broader competitive landscape. The partnership with Disney, which allows Sora users to create videos featuring copyrighted characters and will see curated Sora content appear on Disney+, represents a strategic move to integrate advanced AI capabilities into a major entertainment ecosystem. However, Altman’s emphasis is on the underlying technological advancements that this partnership, and others like it, will help fund and accelerate.

A key insight emerging from the discussion is that while consumer-facing applications like Sora are crucial for demonstrating AI’s potential, the real battleground lies in the fundamental infrastructure and performance of AI models. Altman’s statement about achieving the “cheapest cost per token at the highest level of intelligence” directly addresses this. This implies a focus on optimizing model efficiency, reducing computational costs, and enhancing the raw capabilities of their AI systems, rather than solely on user acquisition numbers. This is a critical distinction for industry observers, signaling that OpenAI is prioritizing a sustainable, scalable, and technologically superior foundation.

The rivalry with Google is also a central theme. Sigalos highlights that despite Google’s Gemini model showing significant user growth, reaching 650 million monthly active users by November, Altman appears unfazed. He dismisses Gemini’s traction as less important than OpenAI’s own trajectory. The strategy, as articulated by Altman, is not about matching Google’s user numbers but about outperforming them on crucial technical metrics. This competitive stance suggests a confidence in their internal development pipeline, particularly concerning model performance and the development of custom AI chips.

The $1.4 trillion commitment to long-term AI infrastructure spending, as mentioned in the report, underscores OpenAI’s ambitious vision. This massive investment is geared towards building the foundational elements necessary for advanced AI research and deployment. It’s a clear indication that OpenAI is playing the long game, focusing on building a vertically integrated stack that can drive down costs and push the boundaries of AI performance. This contrasts with a strategy that might focus solely on immediate market capture through consumer applications.

The Disney deal, therefore, should be viewed through this lens. It’s not merely about leveraging existing IP or expanding market reach; it’s about securing the resources and validation needed to continue their pursuit of cutting-edge AI. The ability for Sora to generate content with Disney’s iconic characters is a testament to the model’s evolving capabilities, but the true value lies in the data and feedback loop that such partnerships can provide, further refining their core AI technology. This move signals a maturation of OpenAI’s strategy, moving beyond the initial excitement of generative AI to a more pragmatic focus on efficiency, performance, and strategic enterprise partnerships.

data.title: Disney-OpenAI Deal: A Blueprint for Creative Collaboration
data.description:
The licensing agreement between The Walt Disney Company and OpenAI, announced on CNBC’s ‘Squawk on the Street,’ signals a pivotal moment for the creative industries grappling with generative artificial intelligence. Disney CEO Bob Iger and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman joined interviewer David Faber to discuss the implications of this collaboration, which centers on integrating Disney’s iconic characters into OpenAI’s Sora text-to-video model. Far from viewing AI as an existential threat, Disney is actively embracing it as a potent tool for innovation and expanded audience engagement, setting a precedent for how legacy media giants might navigate the rapidly evolving technological landscape.

Bob Iger articulated Disney’s strategic rationale, emphasizing the opportunity to connect with consumers in novel ways. He stated, “This is a great opportunity for the company to enable consumers to engage with our characters on what is probably the most modern of technology and media platforms today.” This perspective underscores a fundamental shift from defensive posturing to proactive integration, recognizing AI’s capacity to open new avenues for creative expression and user interaction. For a company built on storytelling and character IP, leveraging cutting-edge AI for consumer-generated content represents a bold move to stay relevant and expand its digital footprint.

A primary concern within the creative community regarding generative AI has been the potential for intellectual property infringement and the displacement of human artists. Iger directly addressed these anxieties, clarifying the nature of the deal. He assured viewers that the agreement involves licensing approximately 200 Disney characters for users to create their own short-form videos through Sora, explicitly excluding the use of character voices or name and likeness of actors. This critical distinction highlights Disney’s commitment to protecting its core assets and the rights of its human talent.

Iger further underscored the mutually beneficial structure, asserting, “This does not in any way represent a threat to the creators at all. In fact, the opposite, I think it honors them and respects them, in part because there’s a license fee associated with it.” This statement reveals a calculated approach: monetizing existing IP in the AI space while establishing clear boundaries and compensation mechanisms. It suggests a potential blueprint for other content owners seeking to leverage AI without undermining their creative foundations or alienating their workforce. The emphasis on guardrails and a “safe environment” for consumer engagement also reflects a conscious effort to maintain brand integrity and trust in an era rife with concerns about AI-generated misinformation and misuse.

Sam Altman, OpenAI’s CEO, echoed Iger’s enthusiasm, praising Disney’s unparalleled storytelling legacy. He observed, “Disney I think is the greatest storytelling company in the world by far, none, and people really want to connect with Disney characters and express creativity in new ways.” Altman views this partnership as a catalyst for unlocking a vast reservoir of latent creativity worldwide. He articulated a core insight of OpenAI’s mission: “One thing we found again and again is that we have underestimated the amount of latent creativity in the world. That if you lower the effort, skill, time required to create new things, people very quickly are able to bring ideas to life.” This speaks to the democratization of creation that AI tools promise, enabling a broader audience to participate in the creative process, albeit within defined parameters.

The collaboration represents more than just a commercial transaction; it’s a strategic alignment aimed at shaping the future of digital entertainment. It signifies a new model where technology developers and content rights holders can collaboratively explore the potential of AI, moving beyond adversarial relationships to forge partnerships that respect intellectual property while fostering innovation. This proactive engagement sets Disney apart, positioning it as a leader in defining the ethical and commercial frameworks for AI’s integration into creative industries.

For founders and VCs in the AI space, this deal offers significant validation for generative AI technologies and a clear signal of market demand from established enterprises. It demonstrates that large-scale IP holders are willing to engage, provided there are robust frameworks for licensing, compensation, and brand protection. This could accelerate investment and development in specialized AI tools that cater to specific content verticals, from character animation to interactive storytelling. It also highlights the importance of building responsible AI systems with inherent guardrails, as trust and safety remain paramount for brand-sensitive partners like Disney.

The focus on short-form video creation, as noted by Iger, further illustrates a pragmatic approach. By initially limiting the scope to 30-second clips, Disney can test the waters, gather user feedback, and refine its strategy without immediately venturing into complex, full-length productions. This iterative approach allows for controlled experimentation and risk mitigation, a valuable lesson for any startup looking to partner with large corporations. The ability to engage particularly younger audiences with Disney characters in new, interactive formats also ensures the brand remains vibrant and relevant across generations.

Ultimately, the Disney-OpenAI licensing deal is a testament to the transformative power of AI and the strategic foresight required to harness it. It’s a bold step that not only expands Disney’s creative horizons but also provides a tangible example of how established industries can responsibly embrace disruptive technologies, turning potential threats into opportunities for growth and deeper consumer connection.

data.title: Disney CEO on $1 billion investment in OpenAI: ‘This is a good investment for the...
data.description:
Disney CEO Bob Iger and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman recently appeared on CNBC’s “Squawk on the Street” to discuss a significant $1 billion equity investment from the media giant into the artificial intelligence research and deployment company. This strategic move signals Disney’s commitment to leveraging cutting-edge AI technology to enhance its vast creative and business operations. The interview provided a window into the motivations behind this substantial investment and the potential implications for both companies and the broader media landscape.

Disney CEO Bob Iger spoke with CNBC’s ‘Squawk on the Street’ about the company’s recent $1 billion investment in OpenAI and the strategic rationale behind this significant financial commitment. He emphasized that the investment is not merely a financial transaction but a forward-looking strategy to integrate advanced AI capabilities into Disney’s core business. Iger articulated a clear vision: “We’ve been mindful of the significant growth in AI… extremely impressed with the progress that Sam and OpenAI have made… This is a good investment for the company.” This statement underscores a proactive approach to embracing technological evolution, recognizing AI’s transformative potential.

A core insight from the discussion revolves around Disney’s long-standing commitment to protecting its intellectual property. Iger highlighted Disney’s history of being aggressive in safeguarding its valuable assets. He noted their past interactions with other tech giants, including Google, where they have had to issue cease and desist letters due to perceived infringements or lack of respect for their IP. This historical context provides a crucial backdrop for understanding why the terms of the OpenAI deal, particularly its “partially exclusive” nature, are so important. It suggests a desire for a controlled and beneficial integration of AI that respects Disney’s creative legacy.

The investment is also framed as a strategic imperative for Disney to remain at the forefront of innovation in the entertainment industry. By partnering with OpenAI, a leader in generative AI, Disney aims to explore new avenues for storytelling, content creation, and operational efficiency. This could translate into more personalized experiences for consumers, enhanced animation techniques, and more sophisticated tools for content development. The partnership is not about replacing human creativity but augmenting it with powerful AI capabilities.

Sam Altman, CEO of OpenAI, echoed Iger’s sentiment, emphasizing the symbiotic nature of the partnership. He spoke about the profound potential of AI to unlock new forms of creativity and consumer engagement, aligning perfectly with Disney’s mission. The $1 billion investment is more than just capital; it represents a deep collaboration to explore how AI can revolutionize entertainment.

The “partially exclusive” nature of the licensing agreement, as mentioned by Iger, is a critical detail. While not fully exclusive, it suggests that Disney will have preferential access or rights to certain AI applications developed by OpenAI, particularly those relevant to its content and business. This strategic positioning allows Disney to harness the power of AI without ceding control of its core creative domains. It’s a calculated move to gain a competitive edge in an increasingly AI-driven world.

Ultimately, Disney’s substantial investment in OpenAI signifies a bold step towards integrating artificial intelligence into the heart of its entertainment empire. This partnership is poised to redefine how stories are told, experiences are crafted, and audiences are engaged, ensuring Disney’s continued relevance and leadership in the evolving media landscape.

data.title: Runware raises $50M to unify AI models with a single API
data.description:
The infrastructure layer beneath the current AI boom is getting expensive and fragmented. Runware, a startup aiming to simplify the entire deployment process, announced a $50 million Series A round today, led by Dawn Capital. Their pitch is simple but ambitious: provide one unified AI API for all models, promising massive performance gains and cost reductions for developers scaling intelligent applications.

Since its founding in 2023, Runware has quickly become a significant backbone, powering over 10 billion generations for hundreds of thousands of developers. The company argues that most teams deploying AI at scale face three major constraints: fragmented model access, slow inference that breaks user experience, and costs that increase faster than adoption.

Runware’s solution combines custom AI inference hardware with its proprietary Sonic Inference Engine, claiming up to a ten times lower pricing structure and faster performance than standard data center setups. This approach has already attracted major customers, including Wix, Quora, and Freepik, who rely on the platform to power image, video, and audio generation for millions of users.

The core of the offering is the unified AI API, which currently aggregates nearly 300 model classes and hundreds of thousands of model variants behind one consistent schema. This abstraction layer allows developers to A/B test, route, or swap models—whether open or closed source—with minimal code changes.

The economic argument is compelling: Runware promises 10x better price and performance for open source models and 10 to 40 percent savings even on closed source foundational models. For open source models specifically, they claim a 30 to 40 percent speed improvement compared with other inference platforms.

Decentralizing the AI Compute Stack

With the AI inference market projected to hit $70 billion by 2028, Runware is aggressively scaling its platform. A major goal is to deploy all two million-plus new AI models from Hugging Face onto the platform by the end of 2026, making every model available through that single API endpoint.

Crucially, they are also building out “inference PODs”—modular compute units that can be deployed rapidly (in weeks, not years) wherever power is cheap and available. This strategy bypasses traditional, slow data center buildouts, placing compute closer to the end-user and aligning with local regulatory needs.

As CTO Flaviu notes, they are building infrastructure for speed, cost-effectiveness, and redundancy. Ultimately, the goal, according to COO Ioana, is to give teams a single API that removes the need to juggle providers or commit to massive minimums, allowing developers to “focus on product.”

data.title: Disney and OpenAI reach three-year licensing agreement
data.description:
The financial world is abuzz with the news of a significant three-year licensing agreement between The Walt Disney Company and OpenAI, the artificial intelligence research and deployment company. This strategic alliance, announced on CNBC’s “Squawk on the Street,” signifies a substantial commitment from Disney, including a $1 billion equity investment in OpenAI and warrants for additional equity. The commentators highlighted the groundbreaking nature of this collaboration, emphasizing its potential to reshape the landscape of AI integration in media and entertainment.

This partnership positions Disney as a key player in leveraging advanced AI technologies. The agreement specifically allows Disney to utilize OpenAI’s Sora platform, a powerful tool capable of generating short, user-prompted videos featuring more than 200 characters. Furthermore, a curated selection of fan-inspired Sora videos will be made available on Disney+, broadening the platform’s content offerings and engaging audiences in novel ways. This strategic move signals Disney’s proactive approach to embracing generative AI for creative and commercial purposes.

The implications of this deal extend beyond content creation. Disney’s substantial investment underscores a recognition of AI’s transformative power and its potential to drive innovation across the entertainment industry. As one commentator noted, “This is where the technology is going.” This sentiment reflects a broader industry trend where established giants are actively seeking to integrate cutting-edge AI capabilities into their core operations and product development. The move also suggests a strategic pivot for Disney, aiming to harness AI to enhance its storytelling and user engagement strategies.

A crucial insight from the discussion is Disney’s intent to become a “major customer of OpenAI.” This implies a deep integration of OpenAI’s technology into Disney’s workflows, potentially spanning content generation, character animation, and personalized user experiences. The agreement to receive warrants for additional equity further solidifies Disney’s long-term interest and potential for growth alongside OpenAI, signaling a partnership built on shared vision and mutual benefit. This financial commitment also suggests a belief in OpenAI’s future trajectory and its capacity to deliver significant value.

The partnership also brings to light the ongoing competition and intellectual property considerations within the AI space. In a related development, it was reported that Disney has sent a cease-and-desist letter to Google. This letter alleges that Google has been using Disney’s copyrighted works without authorization to train and develop its generative AI models. This legal action highlights the critical importance of intellectual property rights in the rapidly evolving field of AI, particularly concerning the data used to train these sophisticated systems. The juxtaposition of these two events – the strategic partnership with OpenAI and the legal action against Google – paints a complex picture of Disney’s approach to AI, balancing collaboration with the protection of its valuable intellectual assets.

The agreement between Disney and OpenAI is a significant development, indicating a substantial shift in how major media companies are approaching artificial intelligence. By investing heavily and securing licensing rights, Disney is not merely experimenting with AI but is integrating it as a core component of its future strategy. This move will likely set a precedent for other media and entertainment companies, encouraging further exploration and adoption of generative AI technologies. The dual actions of forging a partnership and asserting intellectual property rights demonstrate a calculated strategy to navigate the opportunities and challenges presented by the AI revolution.

data.title: Steve Eisman on AI: LLM improvements will begin to gradually slow but not selling AI...
data.description:
“The big question on AI spending is, what kind of returns and cost savings the massive investments will bring.” This statement, from Steve Eisman, former senior portfolio manager at Neuberger Berman, encapsulates the core tension surrounding the current artificial intelligence boom. Eisman spoke with CNBC’s “Squawk Box” to discuss his perspective on the markets, particularly the burgeoning AI trade, and his own health.

Eiseman, known for his prescient short calls during the 2008 financial crisis, expressed a nuanced view on the current AI fervor. While acknowledging the transformative potential of AI, he cautioned against an overly optimistic outlook regarding the immediate profitability of large language models (LLMs). He posits that the rapid pace of improvement in LLMs is likely to decelerate. “We won’t know the answer for a while, and won’t be determined by change in depreciation schedules,” Eisman remarked, highlighting the uncertainty in quantifying the returns on these substantial investments.

A key insight from Eisman is his belief that the market is currently overemphasizing the immediate impact of AI. He observes that the debate is too focused on whether AI is a bubble, rather than on the fundamental question of how these technologies will actually deliver value. “I think what people are focusing too much on is the whole debate that’s going on is actually much more foundational,” he stated. This suggests a need for a more grounded assessment of AI’s practical applications and their eventual economic benefits.

Eisman also shared his personal investment strategy amidst this AI-driven market. Despite his reservations about the rapid growth trajectory of some AI stocks, he remains invested in many of them. “I own a lot of these stocks too. I own them all,” he admitted, indicating a balanced approach that acknowledges both the risks and the long-term potential. This stance reflects a pragmatic investor who is not abandoning the sector but is keenly aware of the need for careful evaluation.

He drew a parallel to the housing market crisis of 2008, where a foundational assumption proved to be flawed. In that instance, the assumption that housing prices would not decline proved to be incorrect, leading to a systemic collapse. Eisman suggests that a similar dynamic could unfold in the AI space if the assumption that LLMs will continue to scale indefinitely without encountering diminishing returns proves false. “Housing prices can’t go down. Once that assumption collapsed, the whole edifice collapsed,” he explained. This analogy serves as a stark warning about the potential consequences of unchecked optimism.

Furthermore, Eisman highlighted the work of individuals like Gary Marcus, a professor at NYU and a prominent critic of current AI development, who has been advocating for a more nuanced understanding of AI’s limitations. Marcus, who co-founded a company focused on AI, has consistently argued that LLMs, while impressive, are reaching a plateau in their development. Eisman seems to align with this perspective, suggesting that the rapid improvements seen thus far may indeed begin to slow. “Scaling LLMs as they keep scaling will start to lose their efficaciousness,” Eisman predicted. This implies that the current strategy of simply increasing model size and data may not yield proportional gains indefinitely.

The market’s current enthusiasm for AI, Eisman contends, is largely built on an assumption that has not yet been fully tested. He points to the fact that many companies are investing heavily in AI without a clear, quantifiable return on investment. This speculative fervor, he suggests, could be a precursor to a correction if the expected benefits do not materialize as anticipated. The critical question remains whether the immense capital being poured into AI will translate into tangible cost savings and productivity gains across industries.

Eisman’s commentary suggests that while AI is undeniably a powerful force, investors and technologists should temper their expectations regarding the speed and scale of its immediate impact. A more measured approach, focused on the fundamental economics and practical limitations of the technology, is likely to be more prudent.

data.title: Oracle’s AI Dip Signals Shifting Market, Not Fundamental Weakness
data.description:
The market’s immediate reaction to Oracle’s latest earnings report, a sharp downturn, belies a deeper narrative about the artificial intelligence landscape and broader economic sentiment. Stephanie Link, Chief Investment Strategist and Portfolio Manager at Hightower, spoke with CNBC’s Frank Holland, offering a nuanced perspective that challenges the knee-jerk pessimism, suggesting that while the AI trade might be momentarily losing favor, underlying strength and future opportunities abound.

Link’s commentary centered on the surprising market response to Oracle’s quarter, which she viewed as fundamentally robust. “I thought the quarter was actually quite good,” she stated, highlighting how earnings were in line with expectations and, crucially, Remaining Performance Obligations (RPOs) surged. This key metric, representing future contracted revenue, was “up 438% year-over-year, up 15% sequentially,” indicating substantial long-term demand for Oracle’s services. Furthermore, every segment of Oracle’s cloud business—total revenue, infrastructure, and applications—not only “beat expectations” but also “accelerated.”

The disconnect, Link explained, stemmed not from operational underperformance but from a lack of transparency regarding the financing mechanisms for Oracle’s significant capital expenditure. Investors sought greater clarity on how these large-scale investments would be funded. This absence of detail triggered a sell-off, even as the core business showed undeniable vigor in the AI-driven cloud space. Such a reaction underscores the market’s current sensitivity to financial specifics, especially in high-growth sectors where future commitments often outweigh present profitability in valuation models.

Despite the immediate market jitters, Link maintains a long-term bullish view on Oracle. She believes the current dip could represent a strategic entry point for investors. “At some point, Frank, this is going to be a buy,” she remarked, emphasizing the need to let the “dust settle” before making moves. This perspective is vital for founders and VCs who understand that market volatility often obscures intrinsic value, especially when the underlying demand for critical infrastructure like cloud and AI services remains robust. The market’s current valuation of Oracle at 33 times earnings might not be “cheap,” but the astounding RPO growth signals a powerful future trajectory.

This sentiment extends beyond Oracle to the broader AI ecosystem. Link observed, “I just think the AI trade right now is kind of getting out of favor.” This shift in investor preference, she argues, is temporary, suggesting that once the initial speculative froth dissipates, genuine opportunities for growth and investment will emerge. For tech insiders, this period of re-evaluation is critical, separating companies with solid fundamentals and clear long-term roadmaps from those riding purely on hype.

Her macroeconomic outlook reinforces this patient optimism. Link expressed “relief that we got through the Fed,” referring to the Federal Reserve’s recent policy decisions. She interprets the Fed’s commentary as signaling a healthy economy that is “growing better than expected” and likely to “accelerate into next year” due to increasing productivity. This positive economic backdrop provides a stable foundation for strategic investments, even as specific sectors like AI experience short-term recalibrations.

Beyond tech, Link identified the financial sector, particularly banks, as a compelling investment opportunity extending into 2026. This outlook is predicated on several factors: the potential for deregulation, the presence of “excess capital” within banks, and improving “net interest income and net interest margins.” Furthermore, accelerating fee income contributes to a healthier financial landscape.

She specifically cited Wells Fargo as an example, noting its strong consumer franchise and company-specific catalysts like the potential lifting of the asset cap, which would “increase growth initiatives and lower costs.” Her confidence in the financial sector also stems from a belief in the resilience of the economy’s upper tier. “The upper end is doing fine,” she asserted, acknowledging the long-standing “K-shaped” economic recovery where different segments perform unevenly. This sustained strength among higher-income consumers, coupled with favorable regulatory and financial conditions, positions banks well for future performance.

Ultimately, Link’s analysis provides a pragmatic lens through which to view current market dynamics. While the AI trade may be experiencing a momentary cooling, the underlying technological revolution and economic growth remain intact. For sophisticated investors and industry leaders, the current period demands discernment—identifying true value in companies like Oracle, which continue to demonstrate strong operational metrics despite market skepticism, and recognizing the broader economic currents that favor sectors like financials. The message is clear: caution is warranted, but opportunities are ripening for those who look beyond the immediate headlines.
